mappings.rationaleAI: African traditional religions as diverse ethnic religious practices directly constitute traditional religions of indigenous African peoples (B.5.a). Animistic elements address external reality viewed as living presence (B.2.c). Ancestor veneration connects to worship of ancestors (B.4.d). Divination and traditional medicine relate to divination and spiritualistic practices (B.4.e). Spirit-world beliefs with layered divine hierarchies address primitive views of reality (B.2.d) and worship of natural objects or forces (B.4.b).

Shamanism as a cluster of spiritual practices involving altered states of consciousness, healing, divination, and spirit interaction directly addresses shamanism, sorcery, and magic in prehistoric religion (A.2.f) and the role of shamanism and divination in primitive religion (B.4.e).
The history of religion covering shamanism, ancestor worship, polytheism from the earliest periods provides broad context for both prehistoric religion (A) and primitive religion (B).

Myth as traditional narrative encoding cosmogonic and theogonic explanations, analyzed through structuralism and functionalism, directly addresses the nature and function of myth and symbol in primitive religion (B.3).
Chinese folk religion's veneration of ancestors, local deities, and spirits, synthesizing animistic and polytheistic elements, relates to aboriginal religions of Asian peoples (B.5.d).

Ritual types including sacrifice, rites of passage, and calendrical rites connect to sacrifices in prehistoric religion (A.2.c) and sacrifice, purification, and passage rites in primitive religion (B.4.a). Ritual's role in communicating cosmological meanings relates to myth and symbol's role in ritual (B.3).
Religion as a comprehensive concept encompassing indigenous and folk traditions, origins, and classification broadly addresses both prehistoric religion (A) and primitive religion (B).
Polynesian religious traditions rooted in Austronesian migration and shared cultural-religious practices directly relate to religions of the Pacific Island peoples (B.5.b).

The Stone Age's ritual and belief systems, petroglyphs, and rock art from the Paleolithic through Neolithic directly provide the context for prehistoric religion (A) and religions attributed to various prehistoric cultural stages (A.3).

Ancestor veneration and conceptions of personal existence after death relate to burial customs and cults of the dead (A.2.a) and primitive cosmogonies, cosmologies, and eschatologies concerning man's destiny (B.2.d).

Anthropology as the scientific study of humanity provides the methodological framework for the study of prehistoric religion (A.1) and understanding the nature and significance of primitive religion (B.1).
Shinto as Japan's indigenous polytheistic and animistic religion, centered on kami inhabiting natural phenomena, relates to aboriginal religions of Asian peoples (B.5.d).

The cross-cultural typology of deities across Near Eastern, Egyptian, Greek, Roman, and Native American traditions addresses female fertility deities (A.2.e) and dynamistic, daemonistic, and theistic views of the sacred (B.2.b). Polytheistic and animistic conceptualizations relate to animism (B.2.c).
Death's religious, cultural, and psychological dimensions across world traditions directly connect to burial customs and cults of the dead (A.2.a).
Folklore's folk religion, customary practices, and rites of passage connect to the nature and function of myth and symbol in primitive religion and their role in ritual (B.3).

Spirituality as engagement with transcendent meaning and inner transformation broader than institutional religion relates to the nature and significance of primitive religion (B.1) and asceticism and spiritualistic practices (B.4.e).
Festivals rooted in ancient harvest and religious observance function as thanksgiving rituals, connecting to sacrifice, purification, and passage rites in primitive religion (B.4.a).
Meditation as contemplative practice originating in Asian traditions, including trance-like states, connects to the roles of asceticism, shamanism, and spiritualistic practices (B.4.e).
Indigenous American civilizations before European contact, including their religious systems, directly relate to religions of the indigenous peoples of North, Central, and South America (B.5.c).
Mesoamerican indigenous religious systems including human sacrifice, ritual ballgame, and calendrical rites directly relate to religions of the indigenous peoples of Central America (B.5.c).
Oceania's Aboriginal Australian and Polynesian traditions, including the oldest continuous artistic tradition, directly relate to religions of the Pacific Island peoples (B.5.b).
